title: Choosing a Deployment Strategy
description:
## Match the Strategy to the Requirement

A team is releasing a high-traffic API and wants to de-risk a new version. The lead describes the desired rollout precisely:

> "Route about 5% of live traffic to the new version, watch error rate and p95 latency for ~15 minutes, and only ramp to 25%, then 50%, then 100% if the signals stay healthy — automatically rolling back the small slice if they don't."

Which deployment strategy is being described, and how does it differ from the alternatives?

options.text: Canary: a small percentage of real traffic is shifted to the new version and progressively ramped based on observed metrics, limiting blast radius and enabling metric-driven rollback before full exposure.

Blue-green: two identical environments run side by side and traffic is cut over instantly from blue to green, with instant rollback by switching back — ideal for the gradual 5%/25%/50% ramp described.
Rolling update: pods/instances are replaced in batches behind the load balancer, which inherently sends a controlled 5% and then 25% of traffic to the new version while observing metrics.
Recreate: the old version is fully torn down and the new one started, with a brief outage window used to sample the 5% of early traffic for metrics.

explanation: The defining feature here — shift a small percentage of live traffic, observe metrics, and progressively ramp (with automatic rollback of the small slice) — is a canary release. (b) blue-green stands up a full second environment and performs an instant cutover; its strengths are instant switch and instant rollback (flip back to blue), but it does not do a gradual percentage ramp, and it roughly doubles resource usage during the cutover. The 5%/25%/50% framing is precisely what blue-green does NOT do. (c) rolling updates replace instances incrementally to avoid downtime, but the traffic split is a side effect of how many new instances exist, not a deliberately controlled, metric-gated percentage; you cannot cleanly hold at 5% and observe before ramping. (d) recreate causes downtime by design and offers no traffic-based comparison. So: gradual %-based, metric-driven shift = canary; instant cutover with instant rollback = blue-green; incremental instance replacement = rolling.

hints.title: Gradual vs instant
hints.body: Canary ramps a percentage and watches metrics. Blue-green flips all traffic at once and can flip back instantly. The question describes a ramp.

hints.title: Traffic control vs instance count
hints.body: Rolling updates change traffic only as a byproduct of replacing instances; a canary deliberately controls the traffic percentage independent of instance counts.
